stories.headline: Irish peacekeepers in Lebanon now behind Israeli lines as IDF advances south
stories.summary: More than 300 Irish Defence Force personnel stationed at Camp Shamrock in Lebanon are now positioned behind advancing Israeli Defence Force lines as Israel continues military operations in southern Lebanon. The Irish soldiers, deployed as UN peacekeepers as part of UNIFIL, face significant safety concerns due to their location amid escalating conflict between Israel and Hezbollah. The situation reflects broader regional tensions following weeks of conflict in the Middle East, including a major escalation involving Iranian forces and U.S. military aircraft. The Irish government has expressed concern about the safety of its troops and is actively monitoring the situation. Irish diplomatic officials are engaged with international partners regarding the safety and operational status of Irish personnel. The positioning of Irish troops in an active conflict zone represents a significant challenge for Irish defence policy and raises questions about the viability of peacekeeping operations in the current security environment. Military and government authorities are assessing options to protect Irish forces while maintaining the peacekeeping mandate.

stories.headline: Storm Dave brings severe gusts and Status Yellow wind warning across Ireland
stories.summary: Storm Dave, the season's fourth named weather system, has brought a nationwide Status Yellow wind warning to Ireland as severe gusts and coastal gales impact the island over the Easter weekend. Met Éireann forecasts winds of approximately 100 kilometres per hour across the country, with the warning in effect until 2am on Sunday and additional rain warnings for areas including Donegal and Mayo. Travel disruption is expected due to the storm conditions, with ferry services and rail operators warning passengers of potential delays and cancellations. Dublin Airport is preparing for disruptions during what was expected to be the busiest Easter weekend of 2026, though flight schedules have not been significantly impacted so far. Authorities have advised the public to exercise caution, particularly on exposed roads and in coastal areas where conditions are most severe. The storm represents the latest in a series of Atlantic weather systems affecting Ireland during the spring season, indicating active oceanic weather patterns. Emergency services remain on standby for any weather-related incidents, and the public is urged to monitor Met Éireann updates for changing conditions.

stories.headline: Russian shadow-fleet tankers monitored off west coast in Irish exclusive economic zone
stories.summary: A convoy of Russian shadow-fleet tankers has been monitored travelling through Ireland's exclusive economic zone off the west coast, with five vessels observed in formation likely attempting to discourage boarding attempts by international authorities. Shadow-fleet vessels are older, often uninsured ships operated by Russia to circumvent international sanctions and transport crude oil in violation of price caps imposed by Western nations. The presence of these vessels in Irish waters raises questions about maritime security and enforcement of international sanctions against Russia. Ireland's neutrality and maritime jurisdiction mean the country plays a delicate role in monitoring sanctions compliance while maintaining diplomatic relations with all parties. The incident reflects broader geopolitical tensions related to Russia's invasion of Ukraine and international efforts to restrict Russian oil revenues. Irish defence and maritime authorities have been tracking the convoy's movements through Irish waters and monitoring their progression. The situation underscores the intersection of international sanctions enforcement with Irish maritime sovereignty and the country's role in global security matters.

stories.headline: Over 1,400 drivers caught speeding in first 48 hours of Easter Bank Holiday roads policing operation
stories.summary: Irish gardaí have detected over 1,400 drivers speeding during the first 48 hours of an enhanced Bank Holiday roads policing operation that began Thursday at 7am and will continue until Tuesday at 7am. The operation focuses on enforcing speed limits and road safety measures during the busy Easter holiday period when traffic volumes increase significantly on major routes. One motorist was caught driving at 147km/h in a 100km/h zone on the N11 in County Wicklow, exemplifying the dangerous speeding incidents being recorded. Gardaí emphasize that the enforcement operation aims to reduce accidents and fatalities on Irish roads during peak travel times. The Easter holiday period is traditionally associated with increased road usage and travel-related incidents in Ireland. The high number of speeding offences recorded in such a short timeframe underscores ongoing road safety challenges and suggests a pattern of excessive speeding among motorists during holiday periods. Public awareness campaigns accompany the enforcement operation to encourage safer driving practices.

stories.headline: Dublin council plans to treble derelict properties facing levies as crackdown continues
stories.summary: Dublin City Council is intensifying its crackdown on derelict properties by planning to nearly treble the number of sites on its dereliction register that face financial levies. The council aims to boost engagement with property owners while implementing a new tax on derelict properties as part of a broader strategy to address Dublin's persistent urban dereliction problem. The derelict property issue has been described as an 'urban scourge' affecting Dublin's city centre vitality and street appearance. The council is testing a new corporate approach on two specific streets: North Frederick Street and Middle Abbey Street, which will serve as pilot areas for the initiative. The strategy reflects growing frustration with vacant buildings affecting Dublin's townscape and local communities, particularly in the city centre where property values are high. Property owners may face financial consequences if they fail to bring derelict buildings back into productive use, whether through renovation or redevelopment. The initiative represents a significant shift in Dublin's approach to urban renewal and property management, potentially generating revenue for further regeneration efforts.

stories.headline: Four charged in court over €7 million cannabis seizure in Co Kildare
stories.summary: Four individuals have appeared in court charged following a €7 million cannabis seizure operation in County Kildare, facing allegations of possessing cannabis and possessing it for sale or supply. The significant drug haul represents one of the largest cannabis seizures in recent Irish law enforcement operations and demonstrates the scale of illegal drug distribution networks operating within Ireland. The seizure reflects continued efforts by Irish gardaí and specialised drugs enforcement units to combat large-scale drug trafficking operations affecting communities across the country. Court proceedings will determine the circumstances of the seizure and the culpability of the four individuals charged in connection with the operation. The charges represent serious narcotics offences with potential for significant custodial sentences if convictions are secured. The case highlights the ongoing challenge of controlling drug supply chains and protecting communities from the impacts of illegal narcotics distribution. Successful prosecutions of major drug trafficking cases remain a priority for Irish law enforcement as part of broader efforts to disrupt organised crime networks.

stories.headline: Family of missing Laois man William Delaney appeals for information after seven years
stories.summary: The sister of William Delaney, who has been missing since 2019 and is presumed murdered, has made an emotional appeal for public information about his disappearance. The case has caused a 'living nightmare every day' for the family, who continue seeking answers about what happened to their brother after seven years without resolution. An Garda Síochána maintains the investigation and actively encourages anyone with information to come forward regarding the circumstances of his disappearance. The family's appeal emphasizes the emotional toll of the unresolved situation on relatives and seeks to renew public awareness of the case. Missing persons cases typically involve significant investigative resources dedicated to resolution, though some cases remain open for extended periods when evidence is limited. Such high-profile cases often generate public interest that can yield crucial leads from witnesses who may previously have withheld information. The family hopes renewed attention and media coverage may prompt individuals with knowledge of the case to provide information to authorities.
